coredata.dc:description: Caspase-3 is an important biomarker of apoptosis-relevant diseases, so it has great clinical value to determine caspase-3 activity. Herein, an innovative photoelectrochemical (PEC)/colorimetric dual-mode biosensor was established for highly sensitive and reliable determination of caspase-3 activity. The advanced Zn-based metal organic framework (Zn-MOF)-derived ZnO polyhedrons/Bi2S3 nanorods/3D sakura-like ZnIn2S4 double Z-scheme heterostructure was prepared by layer-by-layer assembly on the indium tin oxide electrode (termed as ZnIn2S4/Bi2S3/ZnO/ITO), which offered a strong initial photocurrent. The hollow porous gold/silver nanospheres@amino functionalized graphene quantum dots (HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs) with nanozyme activity were designed as dual-signal converters. In a homogeneous system, the caspase-3-specific peptide was connected on the Fe3O4@Au nanoparticles, and further modified with HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs nanospheres. After caspase-3-specific recognition and shear for peptide, some HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs nanospheres were detached from Fe3O4@Au nanoparticles. The freed HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs nanospheres were introduced on the ZnIn2S4/Bi2S3/ZnO/ITO electrode to trigger multiple quenching signal effects (peroxidase mimetic enzymatic catalytic sediment effect; competing effect of the generated sediment for photoexcited electrons; steric hindrance effect). Simultaneously, the freed HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs nanospheres acted as oxidase mimetics to catalyze 3, 3′, 5, 5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) oxidation. Resultly, the photocurrent response decreased while absorption intensity enhanced, thus achieving the reliable and accurate PEC/colorimetric dual-mode detection for caspase-3 activity, which highlighted the potential practicability of the dual-mode biosensor for real cell sample assay.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of (A) bare ITO, (B) ZnO/ITO, (C) Bi2S3/ZnO/ITO, and (D) ZnIn2S4/Bi2S3/ZnO/ITO electrodes.
(A) XRD pattern and (B) XPS survey spectrum of ZnO/Bi2S3/ZnIn2S4 double Z-scheme heterostructure. High-resolution XPS spectra of (C) Zn 2p, (D) Bi 4f and S 2p, (E) In 3d, and (F) O 1s.

(A) TEM image of AFGQDs. (B) SEM, (C, D) TEM and (E) HRTEM images of HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs nanospheres. (F) HAADF-STEM image of the HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs nanospheres and corresponding elemental mapping images of Au, Ag, C, N and O, respectively.

(A) Nyquist plots and (B) photocurrent outputs based on the electrodes modified with (a) nothing, (b) ZIF-8, (c) ZnO, (d) Bi2S3/ZnO, (e) ZnIn2S4/Bi2S3/ZnO, (f) MPA/ZnIn2S4/Bi2S3/ZnO, (g) HPAu-AgNs@AFGQDs/MPA/ZnIn2S4/Bi2S3/ZnO, (h) 4-CN/HPAu-AgNs@AFGQDs/MPA/ZnIn2S4/Bi2S3/ZnO. (C) UV–vis adsorption patterns of colorimetric reaction system for the caspase-3 detection. (a) Without caspase-3 and (b) with caspase-3. Caspase-3, 1 ng mL−1.
ESR spectra of ZnO/Bi2S3/ZnIn2S4 composite under dark and light irradiation: (A) DMPO-·O2 −, and (B) DMPO-·OH. (C) PEC and (D) colorimetric assay principle of caspase-3 activity.

(A) Photocurrent responses towards various concentrations of caspase-3 (a–j: 0, 0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 5, 10, 30, 50 ng mL−1). (B) Linear relationship between photocurrents and logarithm of caspase-3 concentrations. The error bars are derived from the standard deviations of three measurements. (C) UV–vis absorption curves for different concentrations of caspase-3 (a–i: 0, 0.05, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 5, 10, 30, 50 ng mL−1). (D) Linear relationship between absorbance values and logarithm of caspase-3 concentrations. Inset in D: the corresponding color changes. The error bars are derived from the standard deviations of three measurements. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) Selectivity of the biosensor. Caspase-3, 1 ng mL−1; interferents, 100 ng mL−1; the mixture including caspase-3 (1 ng mL−1) and three interferents (each interferent, 100 ng mL−1). (B) Ac-DEVD-CHO inhibition for caspase-3 activity. (a) Without caspase-3, (b) with caspase-3 and Ac-DEVD-CHO, and (c) with of caspase-3; caspase-3, 1 ng mL−1; Ac-DEVD-CHO, 100 μM. (C) Storage stability, and (D) reproducibility of the prepared biosensor for caspase-3 activity detection. Caspase-3, 1 ng mL−1.
Preparation of (A) the ZnIn2S4/Bi2S3/ZnO/ITO electrode and (B) HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs nanospheres. (C) Schematic diagram of the PEC/colorimetric dual-mode biosensor for caspase-3 activity detection.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Caspase-3 is an important biomarker of apoptosis-relevant diseases, so it has great clinical value to determine caspase-3 activity. Herein, an innovative photoelectrochemical (PEC)/colorimetric dual-mode biosensor was established for highly sensitive and reliable determination of caspase-3 activity. The advanced Zn-based metal organic framework (Zn-MOF)-derived ZnO polyhedrons/Bi2S3 nanorods/3D sakura-like ZnIn2S4 double Z-scheme heterostructure was prepared by layer-by-layer assembly on the indium tin oxide electrode (termed as ZnIn2S4/Bi2S3/ZnO/ITO), which offered a strong initial photocurrent. The hollow porous gold/silver nanospheres@amino functionalized graphene quantum dots (HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs) with nanozyme activity were designed as dual-signal converters. In a homogeneous system, the caspase-3-specific peptide was connected on the Fe3O4@Au nanoparticles, and further modified with HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs nanospheres. After caspase-3-specific recognition and shear for peptide, some HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs nanospheres were detached from Fe3O4@Au nanoparticles. The freed HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs nanospheres were introduced on the ZnIn2S4/Bi2S3/ZnO/ITO electrode to trigger multiple quenching signal effects (peroxidase mimetic enzymatic catalytic sediment effect; competing effect of the generated sediment for photoexcited electrons; steric hindrance effect). Simultaneously, the freed HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs nanospheres acted as oxidase mimetics to catalyze 3, 3′, 5, 5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) oxidation. Resultly, the photocurrent response decreased while absorption intensity enhanced, thus achieving the reliable and accurate PEC/colorimetric dual-mode detection for caspase-3 activity, which highlighted the potential practicability of the dual-mode biosensor for real cell sample assay.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Apoptosis, a sophisticated and precisely regulated procedure of programmed cell death, is crucial to maintain normal physiological metabolism and tissue homeostasis [1]. Abnormal apoptosis is highly associated with several major diseases, such as cancer, atherosclerosis, neurodegenerative disease, autoimmune disease [2]. It has been reported that the activation of caspase proteins in cell is a prerequisite for initiating apoptosis [3]. Among the family of caspase proteins, caspase-3 can specifically recognize and cleave the specific peptide chains containing a tetrapeptide sequence of Asp-Glu-Val-Asp (DEVD) sequence, which is the commonly activated caspases [4]. Currently, caspase-3 has been recognized as a biomarker for monitoring apoptosis and evaluating apoptosis-related disease progression. After years of effort and development, many traditional techniques for caspase-3 activity measurements have constantly emerged, including electrochemical detection [5], Western blot analysis [6], colorimetric method [7], electrochemiluminescence assay [8], and surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy analysis [9]. Although some of these have shown application prospects in caspase-3 activity detection, how to effectively solve the problems of high cost, time-consuming, high technical expertise, low sensitivity, poor accuracy is still a huge challenge. Accordingly, it is particularly important to establish a new approach to surmount this challenge. Photoelectrochemical (PEC) biosensing device, as a promising analysis methodology, has aroused considerable interest on account of its excellent assay performance, benefiting from the complete separation of the input source (light) and the detectable signal (electricity) [10]. Additionally, PEC sensing assay possesses the merits of low cost, effortless manipulation, quick detection, and easy miniaturization [11]. Nowadays, a few of works on PEC assay for caspase-3 activity measurement have been reported. For example, Luo et al. constructed a new self-powered PEC platform for highly sensitive assay of caspase-3 activity, based on CdIn2S4/CdS QDs anode and NH2-MIL-125(Ti)@MAPbI3/Au NPs cathode [12]. Wang et al. fabricated a novel PEC approach for sensitively quantify caspase-3 by multi-functional peptide- and electrostatic attraction-guided excitonic response [13]. Yang et al. developed a sensitive PEC sensor for caspase-3 activity assay using Co3O4–Au polyhedral as a multifunctional signal amplifier [14]. Yang et al. also proposed a new label-free and blocker-free PEC sensor for highly sensitive caspase-3 assay based on bifunctional CC-DEVD-peptide modified nitrogen-doped porous carbon-ZnO nanopolyhedra/CdS hybrids [15]. Zhou et al. presented a novel multisignal amplified PEC sensing platform to detect the activity of caspase-3, which was an antileukemia drug evaluation method [16].

Since the high-performance photosensitive material is the cornerstone towards the analysis competence of PEC biosensor, employing and designing the suitable semiconductor materials require to take into consideration [17]. Yet, for the single semiconductor, photoexcited carriers can recombine in the PEC sensing process, thus severely affecting the photoelectric conversion behavior. Engineering heterostructures can effectively suppress the recombination of photoinduced electron-hole pairs [18]. Double Z-scheme heterostructures with excellent photoelectricity properties, constituted of three stacked or coupled semiconductor components, have shown up prominently in the PEC sensing field in recent years, like α-Fe2O3/MoS2/Bi2S3 [19], CdTe QDs/Bi2MoO6/CdS [20], Co9S8@ZnIn2S4/CdSe [21], CoTiO3/g-C3N4/Bi2O3 [22], TiO2@NH2-MIL-125@CdS [23], and Cu2MoS4/CdS/In2S3 [24]. By reason of the increment in Z-scheme electron transmission routes, double Z-scheme heterostructures feature with more superior photoelectric conversion efficiency in comparison with single Z-scheme heterojunction. Furthermore, different from the conventional heterojunctions with cascaded band structures, double Z-scheme heterostructures supply greater advantages to accelerate the migration of photoexcited charges inside heterostructure units, originating from the unique double Z-scheme electron transfer mechanism. Consequently, double Z-scheme heterostructures with more designability can work as photoelectric transducers to address the limitations on the general introduction of only semiconductor or other heterostructures. Nevertheless, the exploration of double Z-scheme heterojunctions in PEC bioassay has received minimal attention from researchers, so that a large investigation gap demands to be filled.

Despite the superiority of PEC biosensor, the single-mode signal response is vulnerable to various interferences (like experimental environments, discrepant operators, nonstandard operation procedures), leading to the lessened information attainment, poor reliability and precision for actual applications [25,26]. Fortunately, PEC-involved dual-mode biosensor provides a powerful choice for reliable disease marker assay, which can greatly diminish false negative/positive errors and improve the accuracy of medical diagnosis and treatment [27–31]. On the other hand, colorimetric assay owns the characteristics of convenience, cheapness and speedy response, turning into a simple and rapidly developing analysis method [32]. In this study, PEC analysis is cooperated with colorimetric assay to construct a PEC/colorimetric dual-mode biosensor, which will be of great significance for monitoring and diagnosing diseases. In regard to the PEC/colorimetric dual-mode biosensing analysis, the enzyme-mediated catalytic reaction is crucial, since it not only regulate PEC signal magnification but also induces the occurrence of colorimetric process [33]. Nevertheless, natural enzymes are easily influenced by external conditions, due to their inferior preservation stability [34]. Moreover, the separated and purified treatment of natural enzymes from biological substances is high-priced [35]. To conquer these defects, researchers have explored substitutes which can mimic the roles and characteristics of natural enzymes [35]. At present, a variety of materials, including carbon-based materials (like GQDs, CDs) [36,37], metal oxides (like Cu2O, MnO2) [38,39], and noble metals (like Au, Ag) [40], have been utilized as nanozyme, which are featured with remarkable catalytical ability, easy biological coupling, and good biocompatibility. For instance, Gu et al. proposed an accurate PEC and colorimetric dual-mode strategy for sensitive detection of Hg2+ in serum, utilizing AgCuO/Cu2O as nanozyme to transform 4-chloro-1-naphthol (4-CN) into insoluble precipitates benzo-4-chlorohexadienone (4-CD), and catalyze 3, 3′, 5, 5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) oxidization [41]. Inspired by the above researches, in our work, hollow porous gold/silver nanospheres (HPAu/Ag) were modified with amino functionalized graphene quantum dots (AFGQDs) to gain a nanozyme (HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs), with the virtue of easy storage, cheapness, high catalytic activity, convenient bioconjugation as well as superior biocompatibility. Significantly, the HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs nanospheres exhibited peroxidase-mimicking and oxidase-mimicking dual-enzymatic activity, which could trigger multiple quenching PEC signal effects and catalyze colorimetric oxidation reaction, thus realizing PEC/colorimetric dual-mode assay with augmented sensitivity employing the amplified signal strategy.

Based on the above studies, herein, an innovative PEC/colorimetric dual-mode biosensor was developed for highly sensitive determination of caspase-3 activity, based on Zn-based metal organic framework (Zn-MOF)-derived ZnO polyhedrons/Bi2S3 nanorods/3D sakura-like ZnIn2S4 (denoted as ZnO/Bi2S3/ZnIn2S4) double Z-scheme heterostructure and dual-enzyme active HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs nanospheres. As seen from Scheme 1 , the caspase-3-specific peptides (biotin-Gly-Asp-Gly-Asp-Glu-Val-Asp-Gly-Cys, biotin-DEVD-peptide) were modified on the surface of Fe3O4@Au nanoparticles via Au–S bond between Au and sulfhydryl groups from cysteine, which was further connected with streptavidin (SA)-labeled HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs nanospheres by the specific interaction between biotin and SA. In the existence of caspase-3, the biotin-DEVD-peptide was specifically recognized and cleaved by caspase-3, causing the detachment of HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs nanospheres from Fe3O4@Au nanoparticles. Subsequently, the freed HPAu/AgNs@AFGQDs nanospheres solution was quickly acquired by magnetic separation, and then utilized for further PEC and colorimetric assay. The HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs nanospheres with peroxidase-mimicking and oxidase-mimicking dual-enzymatic activity, could induce the biocatalytic precipitation process to quench the photocurrent signal and catalyze TMB oxidation to implement a colorimetric reaction. For PEC detection, the ZnO/Bi2S3/ZnIn2S4 double Z-scheme heterostructure was prepared by layer-by-layer assembly on the indium tin oxide electrode (termed as ZnIn2S4/Bi2S3/ZnO/ITO), to supply an augmented initial photocurrent signal according to the matched energy arrangement and high light-harvesting capacity. When the freed HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs nanospheres were introduced onto the ZnIn2S4/Bi2S3/ZnO/ITO electrode, the HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs nanospheres-triggered multiple quenching signal effects could significantly change the photocurrent of the electrode. Concretely, the multiple quenching signal effects were as follows: i) the HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs nanospheres worked as peroxidase mimetics to effectively 4-CN in the presence of H2O2 to generate 4-CD sediments on the electrode surface, leading to the suppressed interfacial electron transmission, diminishing the photocurrent; ii) the resultant 4-CD precipitates could function as electron acceptors to accept photoinduced electrons from double Z-scheme heterostructure; iii) the steric hindrance of the HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs nanospheres inhibited the diffusion of electron donor, further resulting in the reduction of photocurrent response. For colorimetric analysis, the freed HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs nanospheres acted as oxidase mimetics for catalyzing the oxidation reaction of TMB in the presence of H2O2 to produce blue product (oxTMB), which was accompanied with obvious color evolution and absorption signal of colorimetric solution. Therefore, based on the decreasing photocurrent response and growing absorption signal, highly sensitive and accurate measurement of caspase-3 activity could be achieved by cooperation of PEC mode and colorimetric mode. The developed PEC/colorimetric dual-mode biosensor may have an application prospect for clinical diagnostics, bioanalysis, and biomedicine.
The relevant preparation details of HPAu/Ag nanospheres and AFGQDs were depicted in the Supporting Information.

The preparation of HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs nanospheres involved of the classic Au–N or/and Ag–N bond formation. Typically, 4 mL of AFGQDs solution was mixed with 6 mL of HPAu–Ag nanospheres (2 mg mL−1) solution, generating a harmonious mixture that endured for 12 h under ambient condition. The resulting mixture was subjected to centrifugation to collect product (HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs nanospheres), multiple washes with ultrapure water, and an overnight vacuum-drying process.

In the case of SA-labeled HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs nanospheres, the chemical bonding could take place between the amino groups on SA and the Au/Ag in HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs nanospheres. Specifically, a mixture of SA solution (30 μL, 1 mg mL−1) and HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs nanospheres solution (600 μL, 1 mg mL−1) was incubated under shaking at 4 °C for a period of 12 h, leading to the formation of the SA-labeled HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs nanospheres. The resulting product underwent centrifugation and repeated rinses, and then diluted to 600 μL with 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH 7.4). Ultimately, the acquired SA-labeled HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs nanospheres solution was stored at 4 °C for future use.
Preparation and characterization (Fig. S1) of the magnetic Fe3O4@Au nanoparticles were described in the Supporting Information.

To synthesize the Fe3O4@Au-peptide-HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs hybrids, the Fe3O4@Au nanoparticles (600 μL, 1 mg mL−1) and the biotin-DEVD-peptides (400 μL, 40 μM) were first mixed and shaken for a duration of 12 h at 4 °C. After magnetically separated and rinsed with PBS (0.1 M, pH 7.4), the Fe3O4@Au-peptide hybrids were re-dispersed in 160 μL of PBS (0.1 M, pH 7.4). Then, the Fe3O4@Au-peptide hybrids were incubated with 1 mM MCH for 30 min to prevent the nonspecific adsorption. Next, the blocking Fe3O4@Au-peptide were collected via magnetic separation, rinsed with PBS (0.1 M, pH 7.4), and re-dispersed in 160 μL of PBS (0.1 M, pH 7.4). Afterwards, 160 μL of as-synthesized SA-labeled HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs nanospheres was introduced into above dispersion to incubate for 2 h at 37 °C. Finally, the resulted Fe3O4@Au-peptide-HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs hybrids were magnetically separated, rinsed with PBS (0.1 M, pH7.4), re-dispersed in 320 μL of PBS (0.1 M, pH 7.4) and kept at 4 °C before use.

The Fe3O4@Au-peptide-HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs hybrids were incubated with 160 μL of caspase-3 with distinct contents for 30 min at 37 °C. Since caspase-3 could specifically recognize and cleaved the biotin-DEVD-peptide, some HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs nanospheres detached from Fe3O4@Au nanoparticles. The freed HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs nanospheres solution was then gained by magnetic separation for further PEC or colorimetric assay.

Prior to decoration, ITO electrode with surface resistivity <10 Ω sq−1 was cleaned by acetone, ethanol, and ultrapure water. The ZnIn2S4/Bi2S3/ZnO/ITO electrode was prepared via layer-by-layer assembly approach, which was a conventional approach used to modify electrode as reported elsewhere [42,43]. Detailly, 20 μL of ZnO solution (1 mg mL−1) was coated ITO electrode with a modified area of 0.25 cm2 and dried at room temperature. Subsequently, 20 μL of Bi2S3 solution (3 mg mL−1) was dripped on the ZnO/ITO electrode. After drying at room temperature, the resulted electrode was modified with 20 μL of ZnIn2S4 (2.5 mg mL−1) and dried at room temperature to obtain the ZnIn2S4/Bi2S3/ZnO/ITO electrode. Then, 20 μL of MPA solution (3 mM) was coated on the ZnIn2S4/Bi2S3/ZnO/ITO electrode for 15 min at room temperature. Following the wash with ultrapure water, the carboxyl groups of MPA/ZnIn2S4/Bi2S3/ZnO electrode were activated by cross-linking agent EDC-NHS (10 mg mL−1) for 30 min at room temperature. For PEC assay, 20 μL of the above HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs nanospheres solution was dropped on the activated MPA/ZnIn2S4/Bi2S3/ZnO electrode for a period of 12 h at 4 °C. Due to the bonding of carboxyl groups from MPA and amino groups from AFGQDs, the HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs nanospheres were immobilized on the electrode. Afterwards, the HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs/MPA/ZnIn2S4/Bi2S3/ZnO electrode was immersed in biocatalytic deposition solution containing 10 mM 4-CN and 1 mM H2O2 for 30 min. The finished electrode was utilized to carry out the PEC tests.

For colorimetric detection, 50 μL of the above HPAu-AgNs@AFGQDs nanospheres solution was introduced into the mixture containing 500 μM TMB, 10 mM H2O2, as well as 0.1 M HAc-NaAc buffer (pH 4.5) for 10 min. After completing the catalytic reaction, the solution was used to conduct the absorption test.

The surface morphologies of bare ITO, ZnO/ITO, Bi2S3/ZnO/ITO, and ZnIn2S4/Bi2S3/ZnO/ITO electrodes were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM). Abundant indium-tin oxide particles are found on the bare ITO electrode (Fig. 1 A). In contrast to the bare ITO electrode, there are many ZIF-8 derived ZnO polyhedrons on the ZnO/ITO electrode (Fig. 1B). The average size of ZnO polyhedrons is about 63 nm, which is gained from the corresponding histogram of the statistical size distribution in Fig. S2. When Bi2S3 with a mean length size of about 149 nm (Fig. S3) was dropped on the surface of the ZnO/ITO electrode, the electrode topography alters, and plentiful nanorods covers the electrode surface (Fig. 1C), certificating the successful assembling Bi2S3 nanorods on the ZnO/ITO electrode. When the ZnIn2S4 (approximately 5 μm, Fig. S4) was further coated on the Bi2S3/ZnO/ITO electrode, 3D sakura-like microspheres constituted by lamellar structure can be seen on the electrode surface (Fig. 1D), indicating the modification of the 3D sakura-like ZnIn2S4. Furthermore, elemental mapping images (Fig. S5) plainly exhibits the occurrence of five elements (Zn, Bi, S, In, and O) for the ZnIn2S4/Bi2S3/ZnO/ITO electrode, aligning with the elemental analysis results in Fig. S6. Based on the above analyses, ZnIn2S4/Bi2S3/ZnO/ITO electrode has been successfully prepared.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) technique was exploited to investigate the crystalline property of the prepared materials. As presented in Figs. S7–9, for pure ZnO polyhedrons, Bi2S3 nanorods, and 3D sakura-like ZnIn2S4, the diffraction peaks closely match the corresponding standard pattern of ZnO (JCPDS No. 36−1451), Bi2S3 (JCPDS No. 17−0320), and ZnIn2S4 (JCPDS No. 65−2023), respectively, implying their successful syntheses. For ZnO/Bi2S3/ZnIn2S4 double Z-scheme heterostructure formed on the ITO electrode, the XRD pattern in Fig. 2 A reveals distinct diffraction peaks associated with the three pure materials, attesting the successful preparation of ZnO/Bi2S3/ZnIn2S4 double Z-scheme heterostructure. The UV–vis DRS measurements were carried out to examine the optical absorption performance of the prepared materials. As shown in Fig. S10, after calcination of ZIF-8 polyhedrons (curve a), significant absorption improvement can be seen for ZnO polyhedrons (curve b). Due to the good light-absorbing ability of Bi2S3 nanorods (curve c), it can be observed the strong adsorption in the visible light region for ZnO/Bi2S3 composite (curve d). The 3D sakura-like ZnIn2S4 presents evident absorption in the visible light scope (curve e). It is found that the ZnO/Bi2S3/ZnIn2S4 double Z-scheme heterostructure possesses the outstanding light-harvesting characteristics (curve f), demonstrating the photoelectric conversion efficiency will be greatly boosted.

The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was further examined the composition and valence states of elements in the so-obtained double Z-scheme heterostructure. From Fig. 2B, the XPS survey spectrum clearly indicates the existence of Zn, Bi, S, In, and O elements. The Zn 2p spectrum (Fig. 2C) can be fitted into Zn 2p3/2 (1021.9 eV) and Zn 2p1/2 (1044.8 eV) of Zn2+. From Fig. 2D, two strong peaks centered at 157.9 and 163.3 eV belong to Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2 of Bi3+, respectively, while other two weak peaks located at 160.4 and 161.4 eV are derived from S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 of the S2− [44]. In Fig. 2E, the obvious peaks at 444.6 (In 3d5/2) and 452.3 eV (In 3d3/2) are clearly seen, and the low peak at 441.2 eV can be originated from Bi 4d5/2, which coincides with published work [45]. The O 1s spectrum in Fig. 2F can be deconvoluted into three peaks at 530.2, 531.2, and 532.1 eV, which are assigned to lattice oxygen (OL), vacancy oxygen (OV), and chemisorbed oxygen (OC), respectively. Notably, the presence of OV can facilitate the carriers transport, thus effectively separating the photoinduced charge, which results in the enhanced photocurrent response [46–48]. Accordingly, the above XPS outcomes further demonstrate the successful preparation of ZnO/Bi2S3/ZnIn2S4 double Z-scheme heterostructure.

The morphologies and structures of the prepared AFGQDs, HPAu/Ag nanospheres, HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs nanospheres were explored by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and SEM. The TEM image in Fig. 3 A present that the monodisperse AFGQDs have a mean size of approximately 2.7 nm, and an interplanar distance of 0.21 nm related to (102) facet of graphene. As clearly seen from the SEM image (Fig. S11A), the HPAu/Ag nanospheres with an average size of about 150 nm display highly roughened surfaces, thus validating their porous architectures. In the TEM image (Fig. S11B), it is observed that the HPAu/Ag nanospheres disclose a brilliant center encircled by a darker fringe, which is a proof of their hollow structures. Impressively, such a hollow porous shape has big specific surface area exposed abundant catalytic sites, which can enhance the dual-enzymatic activity. The morphology of HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs nanospheres is similar with that of HPAu/Ag nanospheres (Fig. 3B–D). Fig. 2E depicts the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). There are three lattice fringes corresponding to the spacings of 0.20, 0.24, and 0.21 nm, which can be attributed to the (200) plane of Au, (111) plane of Ag, and (102) plane of graphene, respectively. In addition, five elements (Au, Ag, C, N and O) are uniformly distributed within the HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs nanosphere, as depicted in the elemental mapping images by high angle annular dark field scanning transmission electronmicroscopy (HAADF-STEM) (Fig. 2F). This finding is accordant with EDS result from Fig. S12. The XPS characterization was also conducted, as exhibited in Fig. S13. These discussions reveal the successful preparation of AFGQDs, HPAu/Ag nanospheres, and HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs nanospheres.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the proposed dual-mode biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was adopted to explore the electron mobility. As illustrated in Fig. 4 A, an increase in the electron transfer resistance (R ct) value can be observed when the ITO electrode (curve a, 35 Ω) was assembled with ZIF-8 polyhedrons (curve b, 124 Ω), which results from the low conductivity of MOF. After the coating of ZIF-8-derived ZnO polyhedrons on ITO electrode (curve c, 73 Ω), the R ct value diminishes due to the good conductivity of ZnO polyhedrons. However, when Bi2S3 nanorods (curve d, 153 Ω) and 3D sakura-like ZnIn2S4 (curve e, 187 Ω) were sequentially combined with the ZnO/ITO electrode, the R ct value gradually climbs, because of their semiconductor features. The growing R ct value further demonstrate the successful construction of the ZnO/Bi2S3/ZnIn2S4 double Z-scheme heterostructure. For the MPA/ZnIn2S4/Bi2S3/ZnO/ITO electrode (curve f, 253 Ω), the increased R ct value is found, indicating the suppressed electron transmission rate. When the biotin-DEVD-peptides were cleaved by caspase-3 at a concentration of 1 ng mL−1, the freed HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs nanospheres were collected via magnetic separation. After further modified with the freed HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs nanospheres, the electrode displays noteworthy enhancement of the R ct value (curve g, 304 Ω), resulting from the steric hindrance of HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs nanospheres. It can be found that the R ct value of the resultant electrode augments significantly after the continuous introduction of orecipitation solution comprising 4-CN and H2O2 (curve h, 525 Ω), which reveals that HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs nanospheres are preferable peroxidase mimetics for catalyzing the formation of insoluble 4-CD precipitates to obstruct the interfacial electron transfer. The above analyses can testify the successful construction of the designed biosensor.

To further characterize the biosensor assembly process, PEC measurements for different electrodes were conducted, and the corresponding results were exhibited in Fig. 4B. The bare ITO (curve a) and ZIF-8/ITO (curve b) electrodes show no obvious photocurrent outputs. However, ZnO/ITO (curve c) display a weak photocurrent response, due to the improved conductivity of MOF after calcination. The photocurrent response of Bi2S3/ZnO/ITO (curve d) is significantly improved, which profits from the facilitation of electron−hole pairs separation and the improvement of photoelectric conversion performance. When 3D sakura-like ZnIn2S4 was modified on the Bi2S3/ZnO/ITO electrode surface, the photocurrent signal further greatly enhances (curve e), originating from that the matched energy band structure accelerates the separation efficiency of photoinduced carriers. This phenomenon ulteriorly demonstrates that the successful formation of ZnO/Bi2S3/ZnIn2S4 double Z-scheme heterojunction on the ITO electrode, which can improve the analytical sensitivity of the signal-off PEC detection. As a comparison, the other photoelectrodes are fabricated (Fig. S14). Following the modification with MPA on the ZnIn2S4/Bi2S3/ZnO/ITO electrode, the photocurrent signal decays (curve f). After cleavage of the biotin-DEVD-peptides by caspase-3 with a concentration of 1 ng mL−1, the electrode was further combined with the freed HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs nanospheres, and the steric hindrance effect of HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs nanospheres promotes the electrode to generate smaller photocurrent intensity (curves g). Notably, the photocurrent conspicuously descends after the successive addition of 4-CN in the occurrence of H2O2 on the obtained electrode (curve h). Because the HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs nanospheres commendably work as peroxidase mimetics for producing 4-CD sediments, which restrain electron transfer and act as electron acceptors to strengthen the quenching capability of HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs nanospheres.

On the other hand, the UV–vis adsorption spectrum was utilized to explore the catalytical oxidation behavior for TMB (in the presence of H2O2) by the HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs nanospheres. In the absence of caspase-3, specifically cleaved reaction is not induced, so the colorimetric reaction system remains colorless (Fig. 4C, inset a) and has no evident characteristic absorption peak (Fig. 4C, curve a). However, in the presence of caspase-3, the color of colorimetric reaction system becomes blue (Fig. 4C, inset b), as well as strong absorbance response at approximately 652 nm is observed (Fig. 4C, curve b), attributing to that the HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs nanospheres serve as good oxidase mimetics to catalyze TMB oxidation.
The above PEC and colorimetric results certificate that the designed dual-mode biosensor is suitable for measuring caspase-3 activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanistic investigation of the dual-mode biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: To elucidate the possible migration mechanism of photoinduced charges for the biosensor, Tauc plots and Mott-Schottky plots were adopted to investigate the conduction band level (ECB) and valence band level (EVB) of the three synthesized optoelectronic materials. From the Tauc plots (Fig. S15A–C), the band gaps (Eg) of ZnO polyhedrons, Bi2S3 nanorods, and 3D sakura-like ZnIn2S4 are estimated to be 3.26, 1.41, and 2.45 eV, respectively. From the Mott−Schottky plots in Fig. S15D–F, the flat band (EFB) of ZnO polyhedron, Bi2S3 nanorods, and 3D sakura-like ZnIn2S4 are −0.41, 0.11, and −1.26 V vs saturated calomel electrode (SCE), respectively. For n-type semiconductor, the EFB is related to the ECB [49]. And the EVB can be calculated by the formula: Eg = EVB − ECB. Therefore, the ECB and EVB for the ZnO polyhedrons (Bi2S3 nanorods, and 3D sakura-like ZnIn2S4) are calculated to be −0.17 (0.35, −1.02) and 3.09 (1.76, 1.43) V vs the normal hydrogen electrode (NHE), respectively, which are roughly accordant with reported papers [50–52]. Significantly, the appropriate band gap matching structure among these three optoelectronic materials will contribute to constructing the heterojunctions.

Based on the matched energy levels, a possible double Z-scheme electron transport mode for the ZnIn2S4/Bi2S3/ZnO/ITO electrode is deduced as follows. Upon visible-light excitation, the ZnO polyhedrons, Bi2S3 nanorods, and 3D sakura-like ZnIn2S4 can be excited to produce electron−hole pairs. Subsequently, the photogenerated electrons on the conduction band (CB) of ZnO polyhedrons shift to the valence band (VB) of Bi2S3 nanorods and then react with the photoinduced holes in the VB of Bi2S3 nanorods. Meanwhile, the generated photoelectrons on the CB of Bi2S3 nanorods inject to the VB of 3D sakura-like ZnIn2S4, and the photoexcited holes on the VB of 3D sakura-like ZnIn2S4 can consume the photoelectrons. Consequently, the electrons are accumulated on the CB of 3D sakura-like ZnIn2S4 and the holes are reserved on the VB of ZnO polyhedrons. Afterwards, the accumulated photoelectrons move to ITO electrode, and the retained holes are eliminated by AA to further suppress the recombination of photocarriers, resulting in a large photocurrent. Besides, because the reduction potential of O2/·O2− (−0.33 eV vs NHE) is more positive than the CB of 3D sakura-like ZnIn2S4, the electrons accumulated on 3D sakura-like ZnIn2S4 can be grabbed by O2 to generate ·O2 − radicals. Since the oxidation potential of OH−/·OH (+1.99 eV vs NHE) is more negative than the VB of the ZnO polyhedrons, the holes reserved on ZnO polyhedron can be oxidized by OH− to form ⋅OH radicals. Nevertheless, if the electronic migration direction of ZnIn2S4/Bi2S3/ZnO/ITO electrode is assumed as the classical double type-II or any Z-scheme coupled with type-II mode, the accumulated electrons and reserved holes cannot simultaneously generate ·O2 − and ·OH radicals. Conversely, the ESR experiments in Fig. 5 A and B displayed that when ZnO/Bi2S3/ZnIn2S4 composite was irradiated under light, both of ·O2 − and ·OH radicals exist in solution by using 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) as capture agent. As a conclusion, a new double Z-scheme electronic transfer mode is reasonably identified for the ZnIn2S4/Bi2S3/ZnO/ITO electrode (Fig. 5C). Resulting from this superior electron transfer mechanism, the separation of the photoexcited electron−hole pairs can be greatly promoted, which is beneficial to acquire a dramatically enhanced photocurrent for improving the analytical sensitivity of the signal-off PEC detection.

Following the introduction of the freed HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs nanospheres on the ZnIn2S4/Bi2S3/ZnO/ITO electrode, the triggered multiple quenching signal effects can dramatically decline the photocurrent of electrode. Detailly, the multiple quenching signal effects are attributed to the following reasons: i) the HPAu-AgNs@AFGQDs nanospheres serve as peroxidase mimetics for efficiently catalyzing 4-CN into 4-CD precipitates on the electrode surface, thus suppressing the interfacial electron transfer; ii) the generated 4-CD precipitates can work as electron acceptors to capture the accumulated electrons on 3D sakura-like ZnIn2S4, which reduces the electrons that injects to ITO electrode; iii) the steric hindrance of the HPAu-AgNs@AFGQDs nanospheres restrict the permeation of electron donors, further leading to a reduction in photocurrent signal. The multiple quenching signal effects are presented in Fig. 5C.

At the same time, the freed HPAu-AgNs@AFGQDs nanospheres act as oxidase mimetics to catalyze the TMB oxidation to form blue product (oxTMB), accompanying with clear color evolution and absorption signal of colorimetric reaction system, which demonstrates that HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs nanospheres possesses good oxidase-mimicking activity for catalyzing TMB oxidation. In this enzyme mimetics-catalyzed reaction system, ·OH radicals can be generated from H2O2 by utilizing the catalytic effect of HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs nanospheres, which significantly boost the oxidation of TMB into oxTMB, according to previous report [53]. The detailed reaction mechanism of HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs nanospheres-catalyzed TMB oxidation is described in Fig. 5D. Therefore, the proposed PEC/colorimetric dual-mode methodology can be utilized to achieve the measurement of caspase-3 activity, according to the excellent optoelectronic behavior of ZnO/Bi2S3/ZnIn2S4 double Z-scheme heterojunction and distinguished dual-enzyme activity of HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs nanospheres.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing performance of the dual-mode biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: With the optimized test parameters in Fig. S16, the designed dual-mode biosensor is applied to the determination of caspase-3 activity. Fig. 6 A displays that the photocurrent diminishes when the concentration of caspase-3 increases. Correspondingly, a great linear relationship of photocurrent response against the logarithm of target content is observed at the scope of 0.01–50 ng mL−1 (Fig. 6B), with linear regression equation of I (μA) = − 3.569 log c (ng mL−1) + 34.789 (R2 = 0.9987) and limits of detection (LOD) of 4.3 pg mL−1 based on S/N = 3. For the colorimetric determination in Fig. 5C, when the caspase-3 content increases from 0.05 to 50 ng mL−1, the absorption intensity of colorimetric reaction solution at 652 nm enhances with the increase of caspase-3 content. Fig. 6D exhibits the good linear relationship of absorption value versus the logarithm of caspase-3 content. The linear equation is A = 0.272 log c (ng mL−1) + 0.459 (R2 = 0.9992), and the LOD (S/N = 3) is 11.7 pg mL−1. From the inset in Fig. 6D, it can be observed that the blue color alters from light to deep with the increase of the caspase-3 concentration. Thus, the colorimetric mode shows visual color differences, leading to the more visual analysis.

The linear scope and LOD of the fabricated biosensor are comparable to other previous works. In contrast to the published approaches (like spectrum detection, chemiluminescence assay, and electrochemistry analysis) listed in Table S1, the constructed PEC/colorimetric dual-mode biosensor presents preferable sensing performance with the smaller LOD and the higher sensitivity, which is ascribed to the low background signal and enhanced sensitivity of PEC sensing technology, and HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs nanozyme-mediated signal amplification strategy. In terms of sensing time roughly calculated in Table S1, spectrum detection is less than PEC/colorimetric dual-mode approach. Although spectrum techniques have the faster response, they undergo some drawbacks. For example, fluorescence and colorimetry display the bigger LOD, while the instruments of inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry, and surface-enhanced are sophisticated and professional operators, making the detection costly. Colorimetric detection is simple, and the outcomes can be easily observed by the naked eye, making visual analysis without complicated instruments. Moreover, the sensing properties of the designed colorimetric-mode biosensor are superior to the other colorimetric methods in Table S1, because this colorimetric-mode biosensor possesses the broader linear range as well as the smaller LOD, deriving from the high catalytic performance of HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs nanozyme. On the other hand, the sensing performances of the develop PEC-mode biosensor are better to the other PEC approaches cited in Table S1, as this PEC biosensor exhibits the wider analysis range and the smaller LOD. It may be benefiting from the outstanding photoelectric activity of ZnO/Bi2S3/ZnIn2S4 double Z-scheme heterostructure, which can offer a strong initial photocurrent to augment sensitivity. However, the above works are all single-mode detection with relatively poor reliability. Accordingly, the cooperation of PEC mode and colorimetric mode in our work, with excellent detection limits and ranges, is well-suited for highly sensitive and accurate caspase-3 activity determination.

For the selectivity test, bovine serum albumin (BSA), alkaline phosphatase (ALP) and glucose oxidase (GOX), were selected as interfering substances. As exhibited Fig. 7 A, it is observed that these interfering substances (100 ng mL−1) slightly alter the photocurrent intensity compared to the blank sample. Nevertheless, when caspase-3 (1 ng mL−1) is existent, the obvious reduction in photocurrent response can be found. As for the mixed sample, the decreased photocurrent is also gained. Additionally, caspase-3 inhibitor (Ac-DEVD-CHO) was utilized to inhibit caspase-3 activity for ulteriorly validate the specificity. Prior to reaction with biotin-DEVD-peptide, caspase-3 (1 ng mL−1) is preincubated with 100 μM Ac-DEVD-CHO for a period of 0.5 h. From Fig. 7B, the photocurrent response without caspase-3 is similar with that seen for the coexistence of Ac-DEVD-CHO and caspase-3, unveiling the good inhibition effect of Ac-DEVD-CHO on the caspase-3 activity. The findings indicate the favorable selectivity of the designed biosensor, disclosing that the biosensor has potential ability for screening the caspase-3-related inhibitors. Meanwhile, the as-constructed biosensors were placed at 4 °C for 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30 days to investigate the stability (Fig. 7C). The photocurrent response still retains 96.4 % of the original value for caspase-3 assay (1 ng mL−1), even after storing for 30 days, which reveals the acceptable stability. Besides, the reproducibility of the biosensor was investigated (Fig. 7D). For five independent biosensors, a small relative standard deviation (RSD) of 1.4 % is obtained for caspase-3 (1 ng mL−1) detection in the same conditions, which proves good reproducibility of the fabricated biosensor.

To evaluate the clinical feasibility of the PEC/colorimetric dual-mode biosensor, the analytical property was carried out by determining caspase-3 activity in 100-fold diluted Hela cell lysates. As described in Tables S2–3, for the PEC and colorimetric sensing modes, there were acceptable recoveries acquired among 96.7 − 100.6 %, which indicated the satisfactory assay reliability. Importantly, it highlighted the present dual-mode biosensor might have promising potential to measure caspase-3 activity in actual cell matrices.

In summary, a novel PEC/colorimetric dual-mode biosensor for highly sensitive and reliable measurement of caspase-3 activity was developed by integrating ZnO/Bi2S3/ZnIn2S4 double Z-scheme heterostructure with dual-enzyme active HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs nanospheres. The ZnO/Bi2S3/ZnIn2S4 double Z-scheme heterostructure was constructed via layer-by-layer assembly on the electrode, which supplied initial photocurrent with high intensity. After target-mediated specific recognition, the gained HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs nanospheres solution by magnetic separation were used for PEC and colorimetric analysis. The HPAu-AgNs@AFGQDs nanospheres with peroxidase-mimicking and oxidase-mimicking dual-enzymatic activity, could trigger multiple quenching photocurrent signal effects and catalyze colorimetric reaction. Specifically, the HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs nanospheres displayed the peroxidase-mimicking enzymatic activity to form catalytic sediments, which could suppress electron transport on sensing surface as well as function as electron acceptors to accept photoinduced electrons. Additionally, the steric hindrance of the HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs nanospheres further reduced photocurrent response. Therefore, caspase-3 activity could be photoelectrochemically tested with a LOD of 4.3 pg mL−1 in a linear scope of 0.01–50 ng mL−1. Tactfully, the HPAu/Ag@AFGQDs nanospheres also worked as oxidase mimetics to induce absorption intensity enhancement by catalyzing the TMB oxidation. Thus, caspase-3 activity could be assayed by colorimetric technique with a LOD of 11.7 pg mL−1 in a linear scope of 0.05–50 ng mL−1. Accordingly, the PEC/colorimetric dual-modal detection was realized for caspase-3 activity. This research might contribute a new possibility for developing sensitive and reliable analytical tool for biomarker detection in the early-stage preclinical diagnosis of diseases.
